mainEntity.name: How much does a Range Rover VM Diesel engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned VM diesel engine typically costs £3,200–£5,500 supply only, with the 2.5L variant at the higher end. Fitting adds £900–£1,300 depending on the specialist. Used engines can be found from £800–£1,500, but condition varies dramatically and parts are increasingly scarce.

mainEntity.name: Is the Range Rover VM diesel engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The VM diesel is one of the most polarising engines ever fitted to a Range Rover. A well-maintained example with modern composite head gaskets and a perfect cooling system can be reliable and economical. A neglected one is a money pit. The individual-head design means head gasket failure is the main concern — but with obsessive maintenance, the VM has genuine charm and better torque than a Tdi.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Range Rover VM diesel?

acceptedAnswer.text: The main issues are head gasket failure on the four individual cylinder heads (£1,500–£2,000 to fix properly), overheating from an inadequate cooling system, and parts scarcity — injectors, water pumps, and thermostats are increasingly expensive and hard to source. Many owners ultimately swap in a 200Tdi rather than keep a VM alive.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover VM diesel engine, or should I consider a Tdi swap?

acceptedAnswer.text: Given the parts scarcity and high cost of VM-specific components, many owners choose to swap in a 200Tdi (£450–£800 for a used unit) rather than rebuild a VM. However, a well-maintained VM with modern head gaskets and a sorted cooling system can be reliable. Against a Classic Range Rover worth £8,000–£15,000, a £4,000–£5,000 replacement may be borderline — evaluate the vehicle's overall condition and your appetite for parts hunting.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Range Rover VM diesel engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 2.4L VM engine carries codes VM81A or 11A. The 2.5L VM carries code 14L. The 2.4L was fitted from April 1986 to 1990; the 2.5L superseded it around 1989. Check the engine number stamped on the block.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair a VM diesel head gasket, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The gaskets must be replaced — and ALL FOUR must be done at once. Critically, the heads MUST be retorqued at 1,000 miles post-replacement — a step that is rarely done and a key reason for the VM's poor reputation. Use modern composite gaskets. A full head gasket job typically costs £1,500–£2,000.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Range Rover VM Diesel — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance on any VM — must use modern composite gaskets
Radiator, water pump, thermostat — all must be in perfect condition
Individual heads can be replaced, but parts are expensive and hard to source
Specialist rebuild required — parts are increasingly scarce
Often more cost-effective to swap in a 200Tdi

buyingChecks.title: Buying This VM Diesel? Check These First
buyIf: * Head gaskets replaced with modern composite items and retorqued correctly
* Cooling system in perfect condition — new radiator, water pump, thermostat
* Temperature gauge stays below halfway even on hot days
* You're prepared for the parts hunt — or the engine has been meticulously maintained
avoidIf: * No record of head gasket work — assume it's overdue and budget £1,500–£2,000 immediately
* Any overheating history — the VM does not forgive cooling system neglect
* Hard starting or excessive smoke — injection pump or injector issues may be expensive to fix
* You're not prepared for parts scarcity — many owners ultimately swap in a 200Tdi

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The VM diesel is one of the most polarising engines ever fitted to a Range Rover. A well-maintained example with modern head gaskets and a perfect cooling system can be surprisingly reliable and economical. A neglected one is a money pit that will test your patience and your wallet. With parts becoming increasingly scarce, the smart money often goes on a 200Tdi swap. But if you're a purist willing to do the maintenance, the VM has genuine charm — and better torque than a Tdi.

cta.label: → Compare VM Diesel engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover VM Diesel (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Head Gaskets — Confirm replacement with modern composite gaskets; ask for retorque proof at 1,000 miles
* Cooling System — Check radiator, water pump, thermostat, hoses — any weakness is a red flag
* Temperature Gauge — Should sit below halfway even on hot days; any creep is a warning
* Cold Start — Listen for smooth start; any hesitation or smoke points to injector or pump issues
* Coolant Level — Check reservoir when cold; any low or loss is a red flag — expansion tank sits low
* Service History — Including oil and filter changes, fuel filter, antifreeze strength — VM needs obsessive maintenance
* Parts Availability — Research parts costs before buying; injectors, water pumps are expensive and hard to source
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures and structural corrosion (Classic Range Rover chassis are known to rust)
